formulas.context_text: of the equilibrium neutral model, the relationship between polymorphism (measured by nucleotide diversity, $ \pi $) and the between-population divergence ( $ D $) for the ith gene being considered is $$ \pi_{i}=4N_{e}\mu_{i},\qquad D_{i}=2t\mu_{i} $$ (10.1a) where $ N_{e} $ is the effective population size, and t is the divergence time in generations. Hence, $$ \frac{\pi_{i}}{D_{i}}=\frac{4N_{e}\mu_{i}}{2t\mu_{i}}=\frac{2N_{e}}{t} $$ (10.1b) Because the

formulas.context_text: ) for the ith gene being considered is $$ \pi_{i}=4N_{e}\mu_{i},\qquad D_{i}=2t\mu_{i} $$ (10.1a) where $ N_{e} $ is the effective population size, and t is the divergence time in generations. Hence, $$ \frac{\pi_{i}}{D_{i}}=\frac{4N_{e}\mu_{i}}{2t\mu_{i}}=\frac{2N_{e}}{t} $$ (10.1b) Because the gene-specific mutation rates cancel, under the equilibrium neutral model, the $ \pi/D $ ratio at all loci should be roughly the same, namely $ 2N_{e}/t $ (subject to random sampling). The neutrality index of Rand and Kann (1996), $$ NI=\frac{P_{a}/D_{a}}{P_{s}/D_{s}}=\frac{P_{a}D_{s}}{P_{s}D_{a}} $$ (10.6a) indicates which of these two scenarios occurs. formulas.context_text: Recall (Equation 7.13b) Wright’s (1938b) classic result for the amount of time that a new segregating mutation (with selection coefficient s) spends in the interval $ (x, x + dx) $, $$ \phi(x\mid N_{e}s)=\frac{1-e^{-2\gamma(1-x)}}{1-e^{-2\gamma}}\frac{1}{x(1-x)}dx $$ (10.14a) where $x$ is the frequency of the derived allele. In the limit as $\gamma \to 0$, this reduces to $dx/x$, recovering Watterson's expression for the (unfolded) site-frequency spectrum (SFS) for neutra

Under this model, the expected SFS becomes $$ \phi(x\mid N_{e},\boldsymbol{\Delta})=\int\phi(x\mid N_{e}s)\varphi(s\mid\boldsymbol{\Delta})ds $$ (10.14b)

Hence, the probability that we will score a truly segregating site as polymorphic in a sample of size $ n $ becomes $$ \int_{0}^{1}\left[1-x^{n}-(1-x)^{n}\right]\phi(x\mid N_{e},s) $$ (10.14c)

Formally, the selection coefficient associated with the jth new replacement mutation at locus i is $$ \gamma_{i,j}=\gamma_{i},\quad\mathrm{w h e r e}\;\gamma_{i}\;\mathrm{i s a\; s i n g l e\; d r a w\; f r o m\; a}\; N(\mu_{\gamma},\sigma_{\gamma}^{2}) $$ (10.17a) Because the divergence time, $ \tau $, is a common factor over all genes, this allows information to be borrowed across loci (i.e., all loci contribute to the estimation of $ \tau $), improving power

This model can be more compactly written as $$ \begin{align*}\gamma_{i,j}\sim N(\mu_{\gamma,i},\sigma_w^2),\quad{\rm where}\quad\mu_{\gamma,i}\sim N(\mu_{\gamma},\sigma_\gamma^2)\end{align*} $$ (10.17b) which has three distribution parameters to estimate: $ \mu_{\gamma}, \sigma_{\gamma}^{2} $, and $ \sigma_{w}^{2} $. formulas.label: Formula 10.24a
formulas.subsection: Bayesian Estimators of Sites Under Positive Selection
formulas.context_text: Suppose there are k classes, with each class having a different associated $ \omega $. The posterior probability that a specific codon is in fitness class i is $$ \Pr(class i\mid D)=\frac{\Pr(D\mid class i)\Pr(class i)}{\Pr(D)}=\frac{\Pr(D\mid\omega_{i})\Pr(\omega_{i})}{\sum_{i=1}^{k}\Pr(D\mid\omega_{i})\Pr(\omega_{i})} $$ (10.24a) where $D$ is the pattern of codons for that site in the tree, and the prior $\operatorname{Pr}(\text{class } i)$—the values for $p_0$, $p_b$, and $p_d$—is estimated by maximum likelihood. The case of

formulas.context_text: _0$, $p_b$, and $p_d$—is estimated by maximum likelihood. The case of interest is whether the codon belongs to the class of advantageous sites, $\operatorname{Pr}(\omega > 1 \mid D)$, Pr(advantageous $$ \left|D\right|=\frac{\Pr(D\mid\omega>1)p_{b}}{\Pr(D\mid\omega<1)p_{d}+\Pr(D\mid\omega=1)p_{0}+\Pr(D\mid\omega>1)p_{b}} $$ (10.24b)

Hence $$ \omega=\frac{K_{a}}{K_{s}}=\frac{f\mu_{s}+2\gamma p_{b}\mu_{s}}{\mu_{s}}=f+2\gamma p_{b}=p_{0}+2\gamma p_{b} $$ (10.25a) so that very strong, or frequent, selection $ (\gamma p_b > 1) $ is required for $ \omega > 1 $. Similarly, we can rearrange this equation to solve for $ \gamma $, $$ \gamma=\frac{\omega-f}{2p_{b}}=\

Finally, to connect $\alpha$ and $\omega$, from Equations 10.16b and 10.25a, we have $$ \alpha=\frac{2\gamma p_{b}}{2\gamma p_{b}+p_{0}}=\frac{2\gamma p_{b}}{\omega} $$ (10.25c) which can alternately be expressed as $$ \alpha\omega=2\gamma p_{b},\qquad\mathrm{a n d}\qquad\omega=\frac{2\gamma p_{b}}{\alpha} $$ (10.25d)
